monogr.title: The Busy Body;

A Comedy, in Five Acts; By Mrs. Centlivre. As Performed at the Theatres Royal Drury Lane and Covent Garden. Printed under the Authority of the Managers from the Prompt Book. With Remarks by Mrs. Inchbald.

quotation.p: All quotation marks and apostrophes have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
Quotation marks that indicate block quotes have been silently eliminated.

hyphenation.p: Any dashes occurring in line breaks have been removed.
All hyphens have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
em-dashes have been recorded originally using the —

normalization.p: Page numbers appear at the beginning of each page, no matter where originally placed.
Compositors' catchwords and running titles have been silently eliminated.
& has been used for the ampersand sign.
All other characters, those with accents, non-breaking spaces, etc., have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
Writing in other hands appearing on these manuscripts has been indicated as such, the content recorded in brackets.

category.catDesc: author of main text and of works reviewed, if relevant, using LOC authority name headings.

list.item: Corrected titles in teiHeader to remove all items listed as NOT to be used in the Encoding Decisions, Header section, which I also updated to make more clear.
Added myself (with xml:id) as technical editor to the titleStmt

head.bibl:
Elizabeth Inchbald
REMARKS [on The Busy Body]."Remarks." The Busy Body; A Comedy, In Five Acts; By Mrs. Centlivre. As Performed at the Theatres Royal Drury Lane and Covent Garden. Printed Under the Authority of the Managers From the Prompt Book. With Remarks by Mrs. Inchbald. London: Printed for Longman, Hurst, Rees, and Orme, Paternoster Row, pp. 3-6. The British Theatre; or, A Collection of Plays, Which Are Acted At the Theatres Royal, Drury Lane, Covent Garden, and Haymarket. Printed Under the Authority of the Managers from the Prompt Books. With Biographical and Critical Remarks, by Mrs. Inchbald. In Twenty-Five Volumes. Vol. XI. Busy Body. Wonder. Bold Stroke for a Wife. George Barnwell. Fatal Curiosity. London: Printed for Longman, Hurst, Rees, and Orme, Paternoster Row. 1808. The first performance of this play was staged at the Theatre Royal, Drury Lane on May 12th, 1709. Becca Aeschliman, Laura DeWitt, and Mary A. Waters edited this essay for The Criticism Archive.
______

div.p: When a man follows the occupation of a woman, or a woman the employment of a man, they are both unpleasing characters, if they are guided in their pursuits by choice. But, if necessity has ruled their destinies, they are surely objects of compassion, and mercy should be granted to their want of skill in their irregular departments.
The female author of "The Busy Body," was driven to a poet's calling, by the hardships of her fate.

Mrs. Centlivre's father, was the possessor of a considerable estate at Holbeach, in Lincolnshire,Holbeach is a market town and civil parish in the South Holland District in Lincolnshire, a ceremonial county in the East Midlands and Yorkshire and the Humber regions of England. at the time of the restoration; but, as he was a zealous dissenter, he was, of course, persecuted for the political opinions which adhered to this church: his estate was at length confiscated, and he, with his family, obliged to seek refuge in Ireland.Centlivre's father, William Freeman, was a dissenter and a parliamentarian during the English Civil War, resulting in his persecution upon the Stuart Restoration of 1660. Inchbald draws this account from Giles Jacob's The Poetical Register: or, the Lives and Characters of the English Dramatick Poets. With an Account of their Writings. (1719, vol. I, p. 32).

The authoress of this play was at twelve years of age an orphan:Although there is scant evidence surrounding Centlivre's early life, it is generally believed that her father died when she was three years old, her mother died shortly after remarrying, and her stepfather married soon after that, though the timeline is unclear. and at fifteen, being persecuted on account of her poverty and her beauty, as much as her father had been for his religious and republican principles, she pursued his example; and, flying from her enemies, took shelter in England. England had not the virtue to protect her, either from want or from dishonour. A student of CambridgeThought to be Anthony Hammond. met her, a forlorn traveller, on her way to London; and this b 2
young man, being of an engaging mind and person, prevailed on her (destitute as she was) to accompany him to the university in man's attire, as his companion and friend.

The haste, with which this intimacy was formed, was but the forerunner of as hasty a separation. She, however, remained long enough at the college to learn experience, and to improve her taste for literature.This account was first written by John Mottley, a biographer who knew Centlivre in the later part of her life, in his A Compleat List of all the English Dramatic Poets (1747). Another version attributes Centlivre's arrival to London to her joining a company of actors in Stamford, a town 25 miles from her hometown of Holbeach.

The Biographers of Mrs. Centlivre have not said where she met with her second lover; but it is certain she had the prudence to make him her husband: She had the affliction, likewise, to be a widow, before she was eighteen.

Her deceased husband was a gentleman, and the nephew of Sir Stephen Fox. Her next husband was also a gentleman;—for she married, not long after her widowhood, a Mr. Carrol, who was killed in a duel the year following;—and, once more, she became a widow.Mottley claims that Centlivre was married, or in a situation similar to marriage, with a Mr. Fox, a nephew of Stephen Fox. Mr. Fox died roughly a year later. Mottley claims that Mr. Carroll was killed in a duel a year after his marriage to Centlivre.

It was now discreet to think on other support than such as had depended on the lives of two young husbands, who, having offended their family by a contract of marriage, the mere effect of love, had, on their demise, left their relict in the most indigent circumstances. Mrs. Carrol became an actress;—but, notwithstanding her youth, her wit, and her beauty, she was unsuccessful in that profession.

To avoid the alternative, female profligacy, or domestic drudgery, she now encountered the masculine enterprise of an author. She wrote eighteen plays, of
which, three will preserve her memory:—these are, "The Wonder," "Bold Stroke for a wife," and the present comedy.First performed in 1714, 1718, and 1709, respectively. In total, Centlivre wrote 19 plays.

In this period of her writing, (and, no doubt, its concomitant, fasting,)—the reader will not be surprised that Mrs. Carrol should marry a third time.—She now united herself to a man, whose very title promised her protection from that ancient and modern visitation upon authors, denominated—hunger. Mr. Centlivre was "yeoman to the mouth," or principal cook to queen Anne. Mrs. Centlivre's forecast in these her last nuptials did her judgment more honour than her ambition. She died in 1723, of a disorder, neither so lingering, nor so painful, as starving.Centlivre died on December 1st, 1723, of lingering effects of a serious illness she contracted in 1719.

This comedy of the "Busy Body," which has survived one hundred years, was, by the actors, who performed in it, expected to die on the first night.In its original cast, Robert Wilks played Sir George Airy, a wealthy gentleman in love with the heiress Miranda. According to Mottley, Wilks had such a poor opinion of his character that he believed the audience would not be able to bear sitting through his lines. Wilks admitted his pleasant surprise when the play was extremely well-received by audiences.

The foresight of actors, in regard to the success of new dramas, has been long out of credit—unjustly so—for, although their predictions are not infallible, actors are as frequently prophetic, upon the life and death of a play, as the physician upon that of his patient.

The part of Marplot is the sole support of this comedy.The plot of The Busy Body centers around two pairs of lovers, Sir George Airy and Miranda, and Charles Gripe and Isabinda. Marplot is the ward of Charles' father, Sir Francis Gripe, who forces Marplot to pay interest on the money lent to him by Sir Francis out of Marplot's own fortune, which is controlled by Sir Francis.—A most powerful protector in all, that original character can give. The busy curiosity, the officious good temper, and the sheepish cowardice, of this mean atom of human nature, are so excellently delineated, that he allures the attention and expectation of the auditors, and makes them bear with pa- b 3
tience, the dull, and common place dramatic persons which surround him.

Authors of the past time, and those of the present, have had very different notions of the ties which subsist between parents and children. It is shocking to see how tyranny on one part, and deceit on the other, disgrace most of our old play books.Much of the plot's humor resides in the dysfunctional parent-child relationships in the play. Isabinda's father, Sir Jealous Traffick, strictly guards Isabinda's honor, virtually placing her under house arrest. Meanwhile, Sir Francis refuses to turn over Charles' inheritance from his deceased uncle; furthermore, he attempts to marry Miranda, also his ward, for her fortune. It is to be hoped, that these portraits of unnatural vice have been daubed with such hideous colours, they have reclaimed all fathers, mothers, sons, and daughters; and left to the writers of these days, to paint from nature—parental and filial love.
